title: Respiratory death rate from seasonal influenza, age 65+
subtitle: Estimated annual number of deaths per 100,000 people in the age group. This focuses on respiratory flu deaths. It does not include deaths from other complications of the flu, such as cardiovascular disease.
note: This shows an average between 2002 and 2011, excluding the 2009 Swine flu pandemic season.
caption:
Respiratory death rate from seasonal influenza, age 65+

Estimated annual number of deaths per 100,000 people in the age group. This focuses on respiratory flu deaths. It does not include deaths from other complications of the flu, such as cardiovascular disease.

This choropleth world map displays the average respiratory death rate from seasonal influenza for individuals aged 65 and older, covering the period from 2002 to 2011 while excluding the 2009 Swine flu pandemic season. The legend ranges from 0 to 90 deaths per 100,000 people, utilizing a color scale that transitions from pale yellow for the lowest rates to deep purple for the highest. The darkest purple band, representing values between 80 and 90, is visible in a specific cluster of South American countries, including Argentina, Chile, and Uruguay. Surrounding nations in South America, along with countries in Southern Africa such as South Africa and Botswana, appear in dark pink shades that correspond to the 50 to 70 range. Much of North Africa, the Middle East, and parts of Eastern Europe sit in mid-range pink tones, generally falling between 30 and 50. In contrast, North America, Australia, and most of Western and Northern Europe are colored in lighter orange and yellow shades, indicating rates primarily between 10 and 30. Greenland is marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no data is available. The visual distribution highlights higher mortality rates in the southern hemisphere regions of South America and Southern Africa compared to the lighter shades observed across North America and Oceania.
